report_metadata.stage: T4aN0M0 (Stage IVA, orbital invasion, mixed histological components)
molecular_profile: Mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and primitive neuroectodermal components

treatments.name: Craniofacial Resection with Orbital Assessment + Adjuvant Chemoradiation

evidence_level.rationale: Systematic review and survival analysis (PMC 2021) of SNTCS showed surgery + adjuvant therapy provides significant survival advantage over surgery alone (p<0.001). 90% of patients undergo surgery as primary treatment. Multimodal therapy (surgery + RT +/- chemo) is the most common approach in published series. No randomized trials exist due to extreme rarity (<100 reported cases).

survival_benefit.rationale: Mean 2-year survival rate of 55% for SNTCS overall. Recurrence rate 38% with mean recurrence time 21.3 months. Combined therapy significantly improves survival over surgery alone. For T4a with orbital invasion, prognosis is worse than early-stage. Average survival <2 years. Multimodal approach offers best chance of disease control.

accessibility.rationale: Craniofacial resection requires specialized skull base surgical expertise at tertiary centers. Combined neurosurgical and otolaryngologic team needed. Endoscopic-assisted approaches increasingly available. Adjuvant CRT widely available. Limited to major academic medical centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Major surgery with significant morbidity. Complication rate 25-28% for craniofacial resection. Risks include CSF leak (11%), meningitis, orbital complications, brain injury, wound infection. Skull base reconstruction needed. If orbital exenteration required, permanent visual loss and disfigurement. Sequential CRT adds cumulative toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgery is the cornerstone regardless of molecular profile. Mixed histology with multiple components all addressed by complete surgical resection. R0 margins are the primary determinant of outcome. Applicable to all SNTCS variants.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Open or endoscopic-assisted craniofacial resection achieves en bloc removal of the tumor involving the nasal cavity, paranasal sinuses, and anterior skull base. For T4a with orbital invasion, orbital preservation vs exenteration decision depends on extent of orbital soft tissue involvement (periorbita only vs intraconal invasion). Skull base reconstruction with pericranial flap. Followed by adjuvant radiation (60-66 Gy) with concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy.

key_evidence.study_name: Treatment of Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma: A Systematic Review and Survival Analysis

treatments.availability: Standard of Care — available at tertiary skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Cisplatin-Etoposide Chemotherapy (Neuroectodermal-Directed)

evidence_level.rationale: 74.1% of SNTCS patients receiving chemotherapy received cisplatin-etoposide regimen. Rationale based on SCLC-like neuroectodermal component. ORR of 56% for platinum/etoposide in neuroendocrine carcinomas. 15-year SNEC institutional experience showed 74.6% 5-year OS with this regimen combined with surgery/RT. No randomized data for SNTCS specifically.

survival_benefit.rationale: Median OS 12.7 months for high-grade NEC with platinum/etoposide. When combined with surgery and RT in multimodal SNTCS treatment, contributes to improved survival vs surgery alone. Addresses the neuroectodermal component which drives aggressive behavior. Complete remissions reported in sinonasal NEC cases.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin and etoposide are widely available generic medications. Standard dosing protocols well-established from SCLC experience. Available at all oncology centers worldwide. Can be given as neoadjuvant (for downstaging) or adjuvant (concurrent with RT).

safety_profile.rationale: Standard platinum-based chemotherapy toxicity: myelosuppression (grade 3/4 neutropenia common), cisplatin nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, nausea/vomiting, neuropathy. Etoposide adds risk of secondary leukemia with cumulative dosing. Febrile neutropenia risk requires G-CSF consideration.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets the neuroectodermal/small cell component of SNTCS specifically. This histology-directed approach addresses the most aggressive tumor component. Not all SNTCS have prominent neuroectodermal elements — extent of this component should guide regimen selection.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cisplatin forms DNA crosslinks while etoposide inhibits topoisomerase II, both inducing apoptosis. This combination is the standard for small cell/neuroendocrine carcinomas. In SNTCS, it targets the primitive neuroectodermal component (cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, rosette formation, neurofibrillary background) which resembles SCLC biology.

key_evidence.study_name: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma — Indian Tertiary Care Centre Audit

treatments.availability: Standard of Care — widely available globally

treatments.name: Adjuvant Radiation Therapy (IMRT or Proton Beam Therapy)

evidence_level.rationale: 55% of SNTCS patients received surgery + radiation per systematic review. Adjuvant RT significantly improved survival vs surgery alone (p<0.001). Proton beam therapy for sinonasal cancers showed 3-year OS of 100%, locoregional control 92.9%. IMRT and proton therapy both acceptable modalities.

survival_benefit.rationale: Significant survival advantage with adjuvant RT vs surgery alone. For sinonasal cancers with PBT: 3-year OS 100%, freedom from locoregional recurrence 92.9%. Disease-specific survival 90% at 2 years, 74% at 5 years for skull base malignancies treated with craniofacial resection + adjuvant RT.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at radiation oncology centers. Proton beam therapy limited to select centers (approximately 40 in US) but offers dosimetric advantages for skull base tumors. Referral may be needed for proton therapy. Standard 60-70 Gy fractionation.

safety_profile.rationale: Skull base/orbital RT carries significant risks: optic neuropathy, retinopathy, brain necrosis, pituitary dysfunction, dry eye. Proton therapy reduces dose to critical structures vs IMRT. Late toxicities in 15-22% of sinonasal cancer patients treated with proton RT. Skull base bone necrosis with CSF leak in 11%.

biomarker_match.rationale: Adjuvant RT addresses all histological components of SNTCS (epithelial, mesenchymal, neuroectodermal). Not biomarker-dependent. Applicable regardless of molecular profile. Proton therapy particularly advantageous for orbital-preserving approaches to minimize contralateral eye dose.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Adjuvant radiation delivers tumoricidal doses (60-70 Gy) to the surgical bed and at-risk margins to eliminate microscopic residual disease. IMRT uses intensity modulation to conform dose to the target while sparing critical structures. Proton beam therapy exploits the Bragg peak to deliver maximal dose at depth with minimal exit dose, particularly advantageous for skull base tumors adjacent to eyes, brain, and optic nerves.

key_evidence.study_name: Long-term Outcomes from Proton Therapy for Sinonasal Cancers
key_evidence.journal: PMC / International Journal of Radiation Oncology

treatments.availability: Standard of Care — IMRT widely available; Proton therapy at select centers

treatments.name: Histology-Directed Multiagent Chemotherapy (Cisplatin/Ifosfamide/Etoposide)

evidence_level.rationale: Case report rationale (PubMed 2006) proposed histology-specific multidrug chemotherapy addressing multiple SNTCS components: cisplatin for carcinoma, ifosfamide for sarcoma, etoposide for neuroectodermal. Individualized approach based on dominant histological components. Expert opinion level evidence. Logical but unvalidated.

survival_benefit.rationale: Theoretical advantage of addressing all three malignant components simultaneously. Individualized approach may improve response vs single-pathway chemotherapy. Chemotherapy-induced neuronal maturation observed in SNTCS (unique biological phenomenon). No comparative survival data available.

accessibility.rationale: All three drugs (cisplatin, ifosfamide, etoposide) are widely available generic medications. ICE regimen well-established for other sarcomas and neuroendocrine tumors. However, use in SNTCS is off-label and requires oncologist familiar with this rare entity.

safety_profile.rationale: Triple-agent chemotherapy has significant toxicity. Ifosfamide adds hemorrhagic cystitis risk (requires mesna), neurotoxicity, nephrotoxicity. Combined with cisplatin nephrotoxicity and etoposide myelosuppression. Cumulative toxicity is substantial. Requires aggressive supportive care.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly addresses the mixed histology of SNTCS: cisplatin for epithelial/carcinoma component, ifosfamide for mesenchymal/sarcoma component, etoposide for neuroectodermal/SCLC-like component. Histology-directed rather than biomarker-directed. Tailored to tumor biology.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Histology-directed multidrug chemotherapy: cisplatin (DNA crosslinks targeting epithelial/carcinoma component), ifosfamide (alkylating agent targeting mesenchymal/sarcoma component), etoposide (topoisomerase II inhibitor targeting neuroectodermal/primitive component). Each agent addresses a different malignant element of the mixed tumor. Can be given as neoadjuvant for downstaging or adjuvant.

key_evidence.study_name: Rationale for Individualized Therapy in Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma: Case Report

biomarker_requirements: Histological confirmation of mixed epithelial, mesenchymal, and neuroectodermal components
treatments.availability: Off-label — individualized regimen at specialized centers

treatments.name: Orbital Preservation with Induction Chemotherapy and Adjuvant RT

evidence_level.rationale: Meta-analysis reported 83.3% orbital preservation rate following induction chemotherapy with no change in OS. IHNSG guidelines (2025) provide evidence-based recommendations for orbital preservation vs exenteration. Recent study (Eur Arch Otorhinolaryngol 2024) showed 44% 5-year OS with orbital preservation. Response to induction chemo guides orbital decision.

survival_benefit.rationale: 5-year OS 44% for orbital preservation group vs 34% for exenteration group in periorbital invasion. Orbital preservation does not significantly affect survival if periorbita intact. Vision preserved in 61.1% after RT. For SNTCS specifically, orbital invasion pattern determines exenteration necessity.

accessibility.rationale: Requires multidisciplinary skull base team with ophthalmology, neurosurgery, ENT, radiation oncology, medical oncology. Available at major academic centers. Induction chemotherapy (platinum-based) widely available. Decision-making requires institutional expertise.

safety_profile.rationale: Preserves vision and facial appearance when feasible. Avoids disfigurement of exenteration. However, induction chemotherapy toxicity, potential for incomplete tumor clearance if orbital invasion deeper than anticipated, radiation-induced optic neuropathy risk. 27.8% developed severely impaired vision after RT.

biomarker_match.rationale: Response to induction chemotherapy serves as in vivo biomarker for orbital preservation feasibility. If neuroectodermal component responds well to cisplatin/etoposide, orbital preservation more likely successful. Mixed histology may have variable response across components.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Induction chemotherapy (typically cisplatin/etoposide) is administered prior to surgery to downstage orbital invasion. If tumor responds and retracts from orbital soft tissues, eye-preserving surgery can be performed (tumor resection with periorbita removal but eye preservation). Followed by adjuvant RT (IMRT or proton) to treat residual microscopic disease while maximizing orbital structure sparing.

key_evidence.study_name: IHNSG Evidence-Based Recommendations: Sinonasal Tumors Involving Orbit — Preservation or Exenteration

biomarker_requirements: Orbital invasion limited to periorbita (not intraconal)
Response to induction chemotherapy on restaging imaging
No visual loss or extraocular muscle restriction pre-treatment
notable_side_effects: potential need for delayed exenteration if recurrence
treatments.availability: Available at multidisciplinary skull base centers

treatments.name: Tazemetostat (EZH2 Inhibitor) — For SMARCA4-Lost Tumors

evidence_level.rationale: NCI-COG Pediatric MATCH trial (APEC1621C) evaluated tazemetostat in SMARCB1/SMARCA4-altered tumors. SMARCA4 loss is characteristic of SNTCS. Clinical benefit in 38% of SMARCB1/SMARCA4-lost tumors including complete and partial responses. However, overall ORR only 5%. No SNTCS patients specifically studied.

survival_benefit.rationale: Modest clinical activity. One objective response in SMARCA4-lost tumor. Prolonged stable disease in some patients. FDA approved for epithelioid sarcoma (another SMARCB1/SMARCA4-altered tumor). For SNTCS, would be an experimental approach for recurrent/metastatic disease or as adjunct to standard therapy.

accessibility.rationale: Tazemetostat is FDA approved for epithelioid sarcoma and follicular lymphoma. Off-label use for SMARCA4-lost SNTCS possible. Oral administration convenient. Available through clinical trials for SWI/SNF-deficient tumors. Requires SMARCA4/BRG1 IHC confirmation.

safety_profile.rationale: Generally well tolerated. Most common AEs: fatigue, nausea, musculoskeletal pain. Risk of secondary lymphoma (T-cell, 1%). Low rate of grade 3+ AEs compared to cytotoxic chemotherapy. Oral administration suitable for long-term maintenance.

biomarker_match.rationale: SMARCA4 loss is characteristic of SNTCS and is the specific molecular target for EZH2 inhibition. Loss of SMARCA4/BRG1 results in PRC2-mediated gene silencing that tazemetostat reverses. Molecularly rational approach for this tumor's specific genetic alteration.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Tazemetostat selectively inhibits EZH2, the catalytic subunit of PRC2 (Polycomb Repressive Complex 2). In SMARCA4-lost tumors, loss of SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling leads to unopposed PRC2 activity and aberrant gene silencing. EZH2 inhibition reverses this epigenetic repression, restoring normal gene expression programs including tumor suppressor pathways and differentiation.

key_evidence.study_name: NCI-COG Pediatric MATCH APEC1621C: Tazemetostat for SMARCB1/SMARCA4 or EZH2-Altered Tumors
key_evidence.journal: JNCI: Journal of the National Cancer Institute

treatments.availability: Off-label / Clinical Trial — FDA approved for epithelioid sarcoma

treatments.name: Endoscopic-Assisted Craniofacial Resection (Minimally Invasive Approach)

evidence_level.rationale: Pooled analysis showed no significant differences in survival, complication rate, or metastasis between endoscopic and open craniofacial resection. Endoscopic approach provides unparalleled visualization and reduced morbidity. Evidence quality is low due to rarity and heterogeneity of sinonasal malignancies.

survival_benefit.rationale: Comparable survival to open craniofacial resection (94.4% vs 83.3% in one series, p>0.05). Disease-specific survival 90% at 2 years, 74% at 5 years. For SNTCS specifically, limited endoscopic data but technique is feasible for selected cases. Key is achieving R0 margins regardless of approach.

accessibility.rationale: Endoscopic skull base surgery requires highly specialized training and equipment. Available at select academic centers with endoscopic skull base programs. Not all T4a tumors with orbital invasion are amenable to pure endoscopic approach — may need hybrid (endoscopic-assisted open).

safety_profile.rationale: Reduced morbidity vs traditional open craniofacial resection. Avoids facial incisions, brain retraction. Lower wound complication rate. CSF leak rate similar or slightly higher than open approach. Shorter hospital stay. However, for T4a orbital invasion, visualization limitations may compromise complete resection.

biomarker_match.rationale: Surgical technique independent of molecular profile. Goal is R0 resection regardless of approach. Applicable to all SNTCS variants. Selection based on tumor extent and surgeon expertise rather than tumor biology.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Transnasal endoscopic approach provides magnified, angled visualization of the sinonasal cavity and anterior skull base. Combined with image guidance (CT-MRI fusion navigation), enables precise tumor resection with real-time margin assessment. For T4a tumors, endoscopic-assisted (hybrid) approach may combine endoscopic intranasal visualization with limited external access for orbital component.

key_evidence.study_name: Outcome Results of Endoscopic vs Craniofacial Resection of Sinonasal Malignancies: Pooled-Data Analysis

treatments.availability: Available at academic centers with endoscopic skull base surgery programs

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant Cisplatin-Etoposide for Tumor Downstaging

evidence_level.rationale: Indian tertiary care centre audit (ScienceDirect 2024) evaluated neoadjuvant chemotherapy in locally advanced SNTCS. Cisplatin-etoposide most common regimen (74.1%). Neoadjuvant approach aims to downstage T4a orbital invasion to enable less radical surgery. Response guides surgical planning. Case series level evidence.

survival_benefit.rationale: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy may enable orbital preservation if good response. Downstaging can convert borderline resectable to clearly resectable disease. In sinonasal cancers, 83.3% orbital preservation rate after induction chemotherapy. May improve R0 resection rates for T4a disease.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin-etoposide widely available. Standard 2-3 cycle neoadjuvant protocol. Requires close imaging follow-up (MRI) to assess response. Available at most oncology centers. Well-established from SCLC protocols.

safety_profile.rationale: Platinum-based chemotherapy toxicity as described. May delay definitive surgery if complications arise. Potential for tumor progression if disease is chemoresistant. Risk of post-chemotherapy surgical complications. Requires careful timing of surgery (2-4 weeks after last cycle).

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets neuroectodermal component. Response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy serves as in vivo sensitivity test. If prominent sarcomatous component, consider adding ifosfamide. Histology of dominant component should guide regimen selection.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Neoadjuvant cisplatin-etoposide delivered 2-3 cycles before planned surgery to reduce tumor burden, particularly the neuroectodermal component. Restaging MRI after 2 cycles assesses response and guides surgical planning — specifically the decision regarding orbital preservation vs exenteration and extent of skull base resection.

key_evidence.study_name: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma — Tertiary Care Centre Audit

notable_side_effects: risk of disease progression if chemoresistant

treatments.name: Wnt/Beta-Catenin Pathway Inhibitors (Experimental)

evidence_level.rationale: Pathogenetic analysis identified activating p.S45F beta-catenin mutation and constitutive Wnt/beta-catenin signaling in SNTCS. Actionable target but no clinical trials specifically for SNTCS. Several Wnt pathway inhibitors in clinical development for other cancers. Preclinical rationale only.

survival_benefit.rationale: No human efficacy data for Wnt inhibitors in SNTCS. Theoretical target based on molecular profiling. Beta-catenin overexpression is actionable in principle. Would require basket trial enrollment or compassionate use. Entirely experimental.

accessibility.rationale: No approved Wnt pathway inhibitors for any cancer. Several agents in phase 1/2 trials (porcupine inhibitors, beta-catenin degraders). Access only through clinical trials for Wnt-altered tumors. Not currently feasible for routine clinical use.

safety_profile.rationale: Wnt pathway inhibitors have on-target toxicity concerns: bone metabolism effects, GI effects (Wnt essential for intestinal stem cells), potential for growth plate toxicity. Limited clinical safety data available. Early-phase safety signals manageable with dose optimization.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets the identified beta-catenin activating mutation (p.S45F) in SNTCS. Constitutive Wnt/beta-catenin signaling is a validated oncogenic driver. Molecularly rational but clinically unvalidated approach.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Wnt pathway inhibitors target constitutively activated beta-catenin signaling driven by the p.S45F activating mutation identified in SNTCS. Approaches include porcupine inhibitors (blocking Wnt ligand secretion), tankyrase inhibitors (promoting beta-catenin degradation), and direct beta-catenin degraders. Would suppress Wnt-driven proliferation and stem-cell maintenance in the tumor.

key_evidence.study_name: Pathogenetic Analysis of Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcomas Reveal Actionable Beta-Catenin Overexpression

biomarker_requirements: Wnt/beta-catenin pathway activation confirmed by IHC or molecular testing
notable_side_effects: GI toxicity (Wnt role in intestinal stem cells)
treatments.availability: Clinical Trial Only — no approved Wnt inhibitors for solid tumors

treatments.name: Immunotherapy (Checkpoint Inhibitors) — Experimental for Sinonasal Cancers

evidence_level.rationale: Immunotherapy is rarely used in sinonasal cancers and no targeted therapies have been approved. SNTCS specifically excluded from major clinical trials. Molecular basis review (PMC 2022) identified rationale for immunotherapy in sinonasal cancers but no SNTCS-specific data. Combinatory immunotherapy approach tested in sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma may be relevant.

survival_benefit.rationale: No efficacy data for checkpoint inhibitors in SNTCS. Extrapolated rationale from other sinonasal high-grade cancers. PD-L1 status and TMB of SNTCS not well characterized. Would be purely experimental with unknown benefit.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab and nivolumab available but not indicated for SNTCS. Off-label use possible if MSI-H/TMB-H. Access through basket trials for rare tumors. No SNTCS-specific trials available.

safety_profile.rationale: Standard immune checkpoint inhibitor toxicity profile. Generally better tolerated than cytotoxic chemotherapy. Manageable immune-related AEs. Could be combined with standard therapy without prohibitive additional toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: PD-L1 status and TMB of SNTCS not well established. SMARCA4 loss may alter tumor microenvironment. Mixed histology creates heterogeneous immune landscape. MSI status should be tested. Limited molecular rationale specific to SNTCS.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1/PD-L1) restore T-cell mediated antitumor immunity. For SNTCS, the heterogeneous histology may create diverse neoantigen landscape. SMARCA4 loss may alter chromatin accessibility and tumor antigen presentation. Potential for combination with standard chemoradiation to enhance immunogenic cell death.

key_evidence.study_name: Molecular Basis and Rationale for Targeted Agents and Immunotherapy in Sinonasal Cancers

biomarker_requirements: MSI-H or TMB-H for tissue-agnostic pembrolizumab
treatments.availability: Off-label / Basket Trials — no approved indication for SNTCS

treatments.name: Sarcoma-Directed Chemotherapy (Doxorubicin-Based) for Mesenchymal Component

evidence_level.rationale: Doxorubicin-based regimens are standard first-line for soft tissue sarcomas. For SNTCS with dominant mesenchymal/sarcomatous component, sarcoma-directed chemo may be more appropriate than neuroectodermal-directed cisplatin/etoposide. Limited to case reports and expert opinion for SNTCS.

survival_benefit.rationale: Sarcoma chemotherapy provides modest benefit (ORR ~25-30% for soft tissue sarcomas). For SNTCS, would only be appropriate if sarcomatous component is dominant. No SNTCS-specific survival data with doxorubicin-based regimens. Alternative approach when neuroectodermal component is minimal.

accessibility.rationale: Doxorubicin widely available. Standard sarcoma regimens (AD, AIM, MAID) well-established. Available at all oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Doxorubicin cardiotoxicity limits cumulative dose (450-550 mg/m2 lifetime). Myelosuppression, alopecia, mucositis. If combined with ifosfamide (AIM regimen), adds hemorrhagic cystitis and neurotoxicity risk. Significant toxicity burden.

biomarker_match.rationale: Appropriate only if dominant mesenchymal component (rhabdomyoblastic, chondroid, or spindle cell sarcoma elements). Histology review essential to determine component dominance. Not appropriate if neuroectodermal component is dominant.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Doxorubicin intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, inducing double-strand breaks and apoptosis. Targets the mesenchymal/sarcomatous component of SNTCS (hypercellular spindle cell areas, rhabdomyoblastic differentiation, chondroid elements). May be combined with ifosfamide for enhanced activity against sarcomatous elements.

key_evidence.study_name: Histology-Driven Chemotherapy of Soft-Tissue Sarcoma — Review

treatments.availability: Standard of Care for sarcomas — off-label for SNTCS

treatments.name: PTEN-Directed Therapy (PI3K/mTOR Inhibitors) — For PTEN-Mutated SNTCS

evidence_level.rationale: Case report (PubMed 2025) documented aggressive craniospinal recurrence of PTEN-mutated SNTCS. PTEN loss activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. PI3K/mTOR inhibitors approved for other indications (breast cancer, lymphoma). No clinical data in SNTCS. Extrapolated from molecular profiling.

survival_benefit.rationale: No SNTCS efficacy data. PTEN mutation was associated with aggressive recurrence in reported case. PI3K/mTOR inhibitors provide modest benefit in other cancers. Would be salvage option only. Entirely experimental for SNTCS.

accessibility.rationale: Several PI3K inhibitors FDA approved (alpelisib for breast cancer). mTOR inhibitors (everolimus, temsirolimus) approved for other indications. Off-label use possible. Available through basket trials for PTEN-altered tumors.

safety_profile.rationale: PI3K inhibitors: hyperglycemia, diarrhea, rash, hepatotoxicity. mTOR inhibitors: pneumonitis, immunosuppression, hyperlipidemia. Manageable but require monitoring. Metabolic side effects significant.

biomarker_match.rationale: Directly targets PTEN loss/PI3K pathway activation if present. Requires molecular profiling to confirm PTEN mutation. Not all SNTCS harbor PTEN alterations. Molecular testing with comprehensive genomic profiling recommended.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: PTEN loss results in constitutive activation of PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway, driving cell survival, proliferation, and metabolism. PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib) and mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) block downstream signaling to induce cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Relevant only for PTEN-mutated SNTCS subset.

key_evidence.study_name: Aggressive Craniospinal Recurrence of a PTEN-Mutated Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma: Case Report

biomarker_requirements: PTEN mutation or loss confirmed by NGS or IHC
treatments.availability: Off-label / Basket Trials — PI3K/mTOR inhibitors approved for other indications

clinical_trials.title: Tazemetostat in Relapsed/Refractory Solid Tumors with EZH2, SMARCB1, or SMARCA4 Mutations (Pediatric MATCH)
clinical_trials.early_results: Clinical benefit in 38% of SMARCB1/SMARCA4-lost tumors; ORR 5% overall

clinical_trials.title: Tazemetostat for SWI/SNF-Deficient Tumors
clinical_trials.early_results: Expanding on pediatric MATCH data; enrollment ongoing

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Locally Advanced Sinonasal Teratocarcinosarcoma
clinical_trials.early_results: 74.1% received cisplatin-etoposide; feasibility and response data documented

clinical_trials.title: Combinatory Immunotherapy Approach for Sinonasal Undifferentiated Carcinoma
biomarker_requirements: Sinonasal undifferentiated carcinoma or high-grade sinonasal cancers
clinical_trials.early_results: May include SNTCS given shared features with SNUC; combination checkpoint inhibitor approach

clinical_trials.title: Proton Beam Therapy for Sinonasal and Anterior Skull Base Malignancies
biomarker_requirements: Sinonasal malignancy requiring skull base radiation

combination_strategies.rationale: Trimodal therapy (surgery + RT + chemo) provides best outcomes for SNTCS. Surgery achieves macroscopic disease clearance, RT sterilizes margins, cisplatin-etoposide targets neuroectodermal component. Significant survival advantage over surgery alone (p<0.001).

combination_strategies.combination_partner: Surgery with Orbital Preservation Assessment + Adjuvant RT
combination_strategies.rationale: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy enables orbital preservation (83.3% rate in meta-analysis). Response-guided surgical planning: good responders undergo eye-sparing surgery, non-responders proceed to exenteration. Maximizes organ preservation without compromising oncological outcomes.

Triple-agent chemotherapy addresses all three SNTCS components. Proton therapy provides dosimetric superiority for skull base tumors, minimizing late toxicity to brain, eyes, and optic nerves. Maximizes therapeutic intensity while reducing long-term morbidity.

SMARCA4 loss in SNTCS creates EZH2 dependency that tazemetostat targets. Combining with standard CRT may enhance tumor response through epigenetic sensitization. Tazemetostat has manageable safety profile suitable for combination. Requires clinical validation.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Standard of care extrapolation from HNSCC
combination_strategies.rationale: Cisplatin radiosensitization enhances radiation cell kill across all histological components. 20% of SNTCS patients receive trimodal therapy. Concurrent approach provides better tumor control than sequential treatment.

supportive_care.evidence: IHNSG Guidelines 2025; orbital preservation outcomes data
supportive_care.benefit: Comprehensive ophthalmologic assessment before, during, and after treatment. Visual acuity, visual fields, extraocular motility monitoring. Early detection of radiation-induced optic neuropathy enables intervention. Vision preserved in 61.1% of orbital preservation patients.

supportive_care.approach: Sinonasal Irrigation and Nasal Care Post-Surgery
supportive_care.evidence: Post-craniofacial resection care guidelines; rhinology consensus
supportive_care.benefit: Daily nasal saline irrigation reduces crusting, prevents infection, and promotes healing of the surgical cavity. Debridement of the surgical cavity at follow-up visits. Reduces chronic sinusitis symptoms and improves quality of life post-resection.

supportive_care.evidence: Maxillofacial prosthetics literature; skull base reconstruction data
supportive_care.benefit: If orbital exenteration performed, custom orbital prosthesis and osseointegrated implant-retained prosthetics improve cosmetic outcome and psychosocial function. If palatal/maxillary defect, obturator prosthesis restores speech and swallowing.

supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Distress Management Guidelines; psycho-oncology for head and neck cancers
supportive_care.benefit: Craniofacial surgery and potential orbital exenteration cause significant facial disfigurement. Pre-operative counseling, body image therapy, and peer support groups improve psychological outcomes and treatment adherence.

supportive_care.evidence: ESPEN guidelines for cancer patients; NCCN supportive care
supportive_care.benefit: Aggressive trimodal therapy causes significant nutritional compromise. Dietitian assessment, caloric supplementation, and PEG tube placement if needed during concurrent CRT. Malnutrition prevention improves treatment tolerance and outcomes.

supportive_care.approach: Comprehensive Genomic Profiling for Actionable Targets
supportive_care.evidence: NCCN Biomarker Guidelines; molecular oncology standards
supportive_care.benefit: Given SNTCS rarity and mixed histology, comprehensive genomic profiling (CGP) may identify actionable targets (SMARCA4, beta-catenin, PTEN, others) for targeted therapy in case of recurrence. Foundation One CDx or similar platform recommended.
